title: Limit metrics per dashboard card
summary: Generated dashboards pack 6-12 distinct metrics into a single card or viewport, presenting raw data without visual hierarchy or prioritization, overwhelming the user with equal-weight numbers instead of directing attention to key performance indicators.
instruction: A single dashboard card should display at most 3 primary metrics. Additional supporting metrics may appear in a secondary style (smaller, muted, in a sub-grid) but must not compete for visual attention with the primary KPIs. If more than 8 metrics are needed per view, split them into category cards with clear section hierarchy.
rationale: The human brain can hold approximately 4 items in working memory (Miller's Law). Displaying > 8 metrics on a single card forces serial scanning, undermining the dashboard's purpose of providing at-a-glance status. Good dashboards prioritize: a dashboard with 3 well-chosen metrics is more useful than one with 12 undifferentiated ones.
violations: A marketing dashboard card showing impressions, clicks, CTR, CPC, conversions, conversion rate, revenue, ROAS, bounce rate, and time on page — 10 metrics in a single card with no visual distinction.
An analytics dashboard with a single 'Overview' card containing 15 numeric values in a 3x5 grid, all using the same font weight and size.
A server monitoring dashboard where a single card shows CPU, memory, disk, network, uptime, processes, load, temperature, and fan speed — 9 metrics, all equal weight.
controls: A marketing dashboard with three KPI cards: 'Traffic' (impressions, clicks, CTR), 'Conversion' (conversions, rate, revenue), 'Efficiency' (CPC, ROAS) — 3 cards, 3 metrics each, with section headings.
An analytics dashboard with a primary metric card showing 'Revenue: $12.4K (+18%)' in large text, with secondary metrics (visitors, conversion rate) in smaller supporting text below.
A server dashboard with four cards: 'Performance' (CPU, memory), 'Network' (in/out), 'Storage' (used/total), 'Health' (uptime, processes) — each card focused on one category.
borderlineCases: A compact operations dashboard designed for a small screen (smartwatch or mobile terminal) where 4 mini-metrics per card is the maximum for the display size — 4 is stretching but acceptable with clear labels.
contextualExceptions: Embedded analytics widgets where the host application controls the card layout and the dashboard is a raw data display, not a designed interface.
Financial trading dashboards where 10+ tickers on one screen are the expected UX and users are trained to scan rapidly.
User-configurable dashboards where the user has explicitly chosen which metrics to display and can reorder or remove them.
detectorSignal: A dashboard card containing more than 8 numeric values sharing the same font-size, font-weight, and color within a 200px radius
matcherProposal: Identify dashboard-like containers (containers with numeric data patterns: % signs, currency symbols, decimal values). Count numeric values per container. If a single container has >8 values with the same font-weight and within 2px of each other's font-size, flag. Additional check: ratio of numeric values to label text — high ratio indicates metric overload.
detectorLimitations: Cannot distinguish intentional data-dense dashboards (Bloomberg terminal style) from overcrowded ones without context about the user's role.
Numeric values in chart legends, axis labels, and tooltips may be misidentified as card metrics.
Dashboards with expandable sections or drill-downs may show many metrics only when expanded — the initial state may be sparse.
falsePositiveScenarios: A financial data terminal where 15 metrics on a single card is the expected, trained interaction model and users expect density.
A developer monitoring dashboard with 10 server metrics where all are equally critical to the DevOps role.
A dashboard where the 'card' is actually a data table misidentified by the heuristic.
mitigationStrategy: Exclude containers with [role=grid] or [role=table]. Lower to warning threshold at 10+ metrics. Provide data-dense-ok attribute for power-user dashboards.

fixturePlans.violation: A marketing dashboard card with 10 metric tiles (impressions, clicks, CTR, CPC, conversions, rev, ROAS, bounce, pages/session, duration) all at 16px/600-weight/#111827 in a 5x2 grid.
fixturePlans.control: A marketing dashboard with 3 KPI cards: 'Traffic' (impressions, clicks, CTR), 'Revenue' (conversions, rev, ROAS), 'Quality' (bounce, duration) — each card has a section header and 3 metrics in bold, plus optional supporting text in smaller type.
fixturePlans.borderline: A server ops dashboard with a 'System Health' card showing 8 metrics (CPU, mem, disk, net in/out, uptime, processes, load) all in 14px with small labels — borderline but the role demands data density.

cleanRoomAttestations: Developed without access to proprietary prompt collections.
Working memory limit based on publicly available Miller's Law reference.
overlapNotes: First proposal in the dashboards category. No overlap with existing rules.
